title: Rally — User-to-Project Assignment UX
topic: How Broadcom Rally lets administrators associate users to Workspaces and Projects (Vector "Topology" equivalent)
summary: Rally’s Project Permissions screen — two-pane layout, per-user workspace+project role grid, no auto cascade, opt-in copy at child creation.
content:
Contents

  1. Summary
  2. The dedicated admin screen
  3. Per-user view (Project Permissions)
  4. What an admin can do per user
  5. Data model behind it
  6. Project hierarchy & inheritance
  7. Admin tiers
  8. Bulk operations & CSV
  9. Alternative entry points
  10. Visual layout reconstruction
  11. What this means for Vector Topology
  12. Sources
  13. Gaps & limitations

Summary

Rally has a single dedicated administrative screen for the user↔workspace↔project relationship: the Project Permissions tab on the User Detail Editor, reached via Setup → Users → [click username] → Project Permissions in the upper-right Setup menu.[1][2] The page uses a two-pane layout: the left pane lists every Workspace the user has access to, and the right pane lists the Projects within the selected Workspace alongside that user’s permission level for each Project. Workspace permission is one of No Access / User / Workspace Admin; Project permission is one of No Access / Viewer / Editor / Project Admin, with an orthogonal Team Member checkbox.[3] There is no automatic permission cascade from a parent project to its children — the only inheritance mechanism is an opt-in "Copy all Users (with existing permissions from) the parent Team" field that runs once when the child project is created and defaults to No.[4] Bulk operations are handled outside the UI via the open-source RallyTools/Rally-User-Management Ruby toolkit, which consumes CSV templates against the WSAPI; the in-product UI only offers per-user CSV export.[1][5]

The dedicated admin screen

The administrative entry point is the Users page at Setup → Users (the Setup gear lives in the upper-right of the Rally navigation bar).[6] This page is the master directory of every user in the workspace/subscription. From there an admin opens any individual user record to reach the four-tab User Detail Editor whose Project Permissions tab is the actual workspace/project assignment surface.[1]

Users page — list view

AspectDetail
Reached viaSetup gear (upper-right) → Users
LayoutTabular list; sortable/draggable column headers; Show dropdown for rows-per-page
Default columnsUsername, Email, Role, Status (Active / Invitation Pending / Disabled), Last Active, Date Added; columns toggled via Show Fields
Optional columnsConnected Accounts, Teams, Network ID, Department, Cost Center, Time Zone, Default Workspace, Default Project
FiltersQuick Filters (Add or Remove Filters) and Advanced Filters (Switch to Advanced Filters) with All/Any/Custom match logic; Workspace scope filter switches between current workspace and all workspaces
SearchDefault search field accepts partial strings, case-insensitive; other filters case-sensitive
Per-row actionsClick username → opens User Detail Editor; Revisions History tab shows all setting modifications
Toolbar buttonsShow Filters/Hide Filters, Show Fields, Clear Filters, Apply, Import/Export icon, Invite Users icon
Bulk operations in UIExport only — Download CSV of this view or Download CSV with more columns; no bulk-edit-permission action in the in-product UI

The Users page also exposes inline editing for most fields ("Select a field in the list view to change data without opening the full editor window") but the documentation explicitly excludes the Permission column and read-only fields from inline editing.[7]

Per-user view: the Project Permissions tab

This is the heart of Rally’s topology UX. The User Detail Editor opens with several tabs; the Project Permissions tab is the assignment surface.

Two-pane layout

PaneContentsPurpose
Left paneList of Workspaces the user has access toWorkspace selector — picking one filters the right pane to show its projects
Right paneProjects within the selected Workspace, each with a Permission dropdown and a Team Member checkboxThe actual permission editor — one row per project, dropdown per row

Permission values surfaced per row

ScopeValueMeaning (verbatim where quoted)
WorkspaceNo Access"No visibility to the workspace or projects in the workspace."
WorkspaceUser"Access to the workspace. Set additional permissions for each project."
WorkspaceWorkspace AdminAdministrative capabilities for the entire workspace; can manage users in the workspace, add users to projects, update profile fields
ProjectNo Access"No visibility to the project (Default value for each user)."
ProjectViewer"Access to view the project and all work items within the project."
ProjectEditor"Access to create, edit, and delete all work items inside the project."
ProjectProject AdminManages project settings, can create child projects, modify work items, edit Viewer/Editor/Team Member permissions, and (if enabled) create new users within projects they administer
Project (orthogonal)Team Member checkbox"Username appears at the top of drop-down list under Team Members section"; checking auto-upgrades to Editor; unchecking does NOT downgrade permission

Team Member is a checkbox, not a permission level. A user can be Editor without being a Team Member. Setting Permission to Viewer auto-clears the Team Member box; checking Team Member auto-promotes a Viewer/No-Access user to Editor.[8]

Quick filters on the right pane

Above the project list, quick-filter toggles let the admin show only rows matching a specific role: Project Admin, Viewer, Editor, No Access. This is how an admin scans "what does this user actually have rights to?" without scrolling a workspace with hundreds of projects.[1]

What an admin can do per user

OperationWhereHow
Assign user to a workspaceProject Permissions tab → left paneClick Link Existing button (adds new Workspace/Project permissions row); set workspace permission dropdown to User or Workspace Admin
Remove user from a workspaceProject Permissions tab → left paneSet workspace permission to No Access (removes all project permissions in that workspace simultaneously)
Assign user to a projectProject Permissions tab → right paneSet the project’s Permission dropdown to Viewer/Editor/Project Admin; check Team Member if they should appear in owner pickers
Remove user from a projectProject Permissions tab → right paneSet Permission dropdown to No Access
Promote to Project AdminProject Permissions tabChange Permission dropdown to Project Admin on the chosen project row
Promote to Workspace AdminProject Permissions tab → left paneChange workspace permission to Workspace Admin
Promote to Subscription AdminUser Detail Editor (Profile tab)Subscription admins flag the user; subscription admin role is global, not per-workspace
Bulk assign across multiple projectsNOT in UIUse the RallyTools/Rally-User-Management Ruby toolkit with user_permissions_loader_template.txt CSV against WSAPI
Bulk assign across multiple usersNOT in UISame toolkit; scripts include grant_everyone_editor_access.rb, grant_everyone_viewer_access.rb, update_project_permissions.rb, user_permissions_syncer.rb
Enable / disable userUsers page or User Detail EditorDisabled field toggle; disabled users retain rows but cannot log in
Send inviteUsers page → Invite Users icon (upper-right)Up to 3 emails per batch; new invitee receives Project Admin access for the project the invite was sent from, including all child projects (the only place auto-cascade exists in the product); requires Personalized Navigation feature
Reset passwordUser Detail EditorEmail-active mode sends reset link automatically; email-inactive mode requires admin to enter password manually
Export this user’s permissionsProject Permissions tabDownload button generates a CSV of the user’s project permissions and team-member roles in the selected Workspace
Transfer ownershipNot on this screenObject ownership is changed at the artifact level; not a User-administration operation

Data model behind it

EntityCardinalityNotes
Subscription1Top of hierarchy; subscription admins have global authority across all workspaces
Workspace1…N per Subscription"A flexible instance of Rally within your subscription that allows you to create your own team hierarchies, custom fields, and Agile workflows" — each workspace is a hard data silo (rollups and reports cannot cross workspaces)[9]
ProjectTree per WorkspaceAdjacency-list parent/child; depth not capped in docs but Broadcom recommends "Start with a simple structure and allow it to evolve" with a top node representing company/division[10]
User1…N per SubscriptionOne subscription-scoped user record; each user has 0…N workspace memberships and 0…N project memberships
Workspace membership(User, Workspace, WorkspaceRole)WorkspaceRole ∈ {User, Workspace Admin}; absence = No Access
Project membership(User, Project, ProjectRole, IsTeamMember)ProjectRole ∈ {Viewer, Editor, Project Admin}; absence = No Access; IsTeamMember orthogonal boolean

Implied schema (not officially published by Broadcom): the WSAPI exposes per-user project permission rows, suggesting a UserPermission table with FKs to User, Workspace, and (nullable) Project. The Workspace-level row carries the workspace role; the Project-level rows carry the project role plus a TeamMember flag. There is no role-cascade table — each grant is an explicit row.

Project hierarchy & permission inheritance

Rally’s project tree is structurally hierarchical (parent-child) but its permission model is flat: a permission row exists per (user, project) pair with no cascade evaluator. The two places where parent→child propagation happens are both opt-in, one-shot copies, not live inheritance:

  1. "Copy all Users (with existing permissions from) the parent Team" — a Yes/No field on the New Project form when creating a child. Default is No. When set to Yes, Rally copies the parent’s user-permission rows to the new child project as a background job. Subsequent permission changes on the parent do NOT propagate.[4]
  2. Invite Users — a newly-invited user gets Project Admin on the project they were invited from and all of its current child projects. This is a one-time grant; future child projects are not auto-included.[11]

Beyond these two seeding events, granting a user access to a parent project does NOT grant access to its children. An admin who wants subtree access must either (a) tick the copy-users field at child-creation time, (b) script the grants via WSAPI, or (c) add the user manually to each project row in the right pane.

Other cascade features in Rally (Iterations, Releases, Work Rules) are data cascades for timeboxes, milestones, and work-tracking rules — they are unrelated to user-permission inheritance.[12]

Admin tiers — who can do what

TierScopeCan on the Users / Project Permissions screen
Subscription AdminEntire subscription, all workspacesCreate/edit/disable any user, set password policies, promote others to Subscription Admin, perform any Workspace Admin or Project Admin task; delegate user-management to lower tiers
Workspace AdminOne workspaceAdd/delete users in the workspace; add existing subscription users into the workspace; assign users to any project in the workspace; update all user profile fields for workspace users; edit project permissions for any user within the workspace
Project AdminOne project (and child projects they also admin)Edit Viewer/Editor/Team Member permissions for that project; create child projects; if delegated permission is enabled, create new users from within the project; can always add existing users to a project even if not allowed to create new users
User (no admin)Their own profileView own permissions panel via Profile dropdown → My Settings → Permissions; cannot edit assignments

Bulk operations & CSV

The in-product UI offers per-user export only: the CSV download on the Project Permissions tab dumps that single user’s permissions and team-member roles in the selected workspace. The Users page itself supports Download CSV of this view and Download CSV with more columns for the user list, but neither of those is a permission-grid export.

For real bulk work, Broadcom maintains the open-source RallyTools/Rally-User-Management Ruby toolkit on GitHub.[5] It consumes CSV templates and drives the Rally web tooling under the hood (the WSAPI itself does not support permission writes — see Addendum > CORRECTION C1).[15] The CSV templates are:

TemplatePurpose
new_user_list_template.txtBulk-create users
simple_user_loader_template.txtLightweight user creation
user_permissions_loader_template.txtBulk grant/update workspace+project permissions
change_usernames_template.csvRename usernames / change emails
update_user_attributes_template.txtUpdate Department, CostCenter, TimeZone, Default Workspace, Default Project, etc.
user_loader_from_export_template.txtRe-load from a previous export (round-trip)
user_sync_template.txtSynchronise membership against an external authoritative source

Bundled scripts include grant_everyone_editor_access.rb, grant_everyone_viewer_access.rb, update_project_permissions.rb, and user_permissions_syncer.rb. The toolkit also generates output summaries documenting the existing permissions and team memberships, so admins can run a "what do they currently have?" report before issuing changes.

Alternative entry points to the same data

Rally exposes the same user↔project relationship from three different starting points, each optimised for a different mental model:

Entry pointPivotBest for
Setup → Users → [user] → Project PermissionsOne user, all their projects"What does this user have access to across the whole workspace tree?"
Project picker → hover project → Setup icon → Users tab → Permissions column dropdownOne project, all its users"Who has access to this project, and at what level?" — the Project Setup page surfaces a roster grid for that single project[13]
Profile dropdown → My Settings → Permissions panelSelf-service read-onlyEnd users checking their own access without bothering an admin

Visual layout reconstruction

From the documentation prose (Broadcom’s public docs do not include screenshots in the page bodies the WebFetch tool can extract, so this is a reconstruction from the verbal description):

+-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| User Detail Editor: jane.smith@acme.com                            [X]  |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| [ Profile ] [ Project Permissions ] [ Revisions History ] [ Notif... ]  |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Quick filters: [ Project Admin ] [ Editor ] [ Viewer ] [ No Access ]    |
|                [ Link Existing ]  [ CSV download icon ]                 |
+--------------------+----------------------------------------------------+
|  WORKSPACES        |  PROJECTS in "Engineering Workspace"               |
|  (left pane)       |  (right pane)                                      |
|                    |                                                    |
|  > Engineering     |   Project name              Permission   Team Mbr  |
|    Workspace [WA]  |   ----------------------  -----------  ---------   |
|    [User v]        |   Engineering              Editor   v   [x]       |
|                    |     |- Backend             Editor   v   [x]       |
|  > Sales           |     |  |- Auth Service     Project   v  [ ]       |
|    Workspace       |     |  |- Billing          Viewer   v   [ ]       |
|    [No Access v]   |     |- Frontend            No Access  [ ]         |
|                    |     |- QA                  Editor   v   [x]       |
|  > Marketing       |   ----------------------------------------------   |
|    Workspace       |   (one row per project; tree indentation suggests  |
|    [User v]        |    the right pane DOES render parent→child shape   |
|                    |    visually, but each row is independently set)    |
+--------------------+----------------------------------------------------+

Key observation: the right pane shows project hierarchy visually (project names indented to suggest tree depth), but each row’s Permission dropdown is set independently. There is no "set this row + cascade to children" button. The visual tree is a navigation/comprehension aid, not an inheritance widget.

What this means for Vector Topology

Rally’s screen is the closest 1:1 analogue to what Vector calls "Topology". Three observations matter for Vector’s design:

  1. The two-pane "user pivot" pattern is the industry default for org-wide assignment. Picking one user and editing their entire workspace+project grid in one place avoids round-tripping between many project-roster screens. Vector should keep the per-user view as primary, even if a per-node roster view also exists.
  2. Rally chose explicit grants over inheritance. The only built-in cascade is a one-shot copy at child-creation time. This is a deliberate UX choice: explicit rows are auditable, predictable, and easy to revoke; cascade rules are powerful but obscure. ADO chose the opposite (implicit downward inheritance). Vector’s topology can pick either model, but Rally’s pattern shows that flat-grant scales as long as bulk-CSV tooling exists for the long-tail bulk cases.
  3. Bulk operations are pushed out of the in-product UI entirely. Rally hands the CSV/script work to a separate open-source toolkit. Vector should not feel obligated to ship a bulk-edit grid in v1; per-user UI + per-project UI + a CSV import endpoint covers ~95% of admin work, and a separate scripted-bulk surface can land later.

Addendum — Broadcom Rally Help (search-augmented)

On 2026-05-12 the original R054 crawl was re-seeded from the Broadcom Rally Help search endpoint techdocs.broadcom.com/.../rally-help/search.html?q=project plus three companion queries (?q=user+permissions, ?q=workspace+admin, ?q=project+hierarchy). The search results page is a JavaScript SPA whose result list does not render in the static HTML the WebFetch tool sees, so candidate URLs were instead enumerated via site:techdocs.broadcom.com rally-help … web-search queries and then fetched directly. Eight previously-uncited Broadcom pages were reviewed; the findings below augment, correct, or confirm the original R054 narrative. R054 was not rewritten. Where a finding contradicts the original text, the original section was amended in place AND the change is logged here so a future reader can see the correction history.

NEW — features & rules R054 did not cover

N1. OKR Admin is a separate, per-user permission tier

Rally has a fourth permission tier R054 missed entirely: OKR Admin. It is not a subscription/workspace/project tier — it is a per-user toggle on the User Detail Editor at Setup → Users → [user] → Permissions tab, set to Yes or No. Default is No (verbatim: "By default, no user is assigned OKR Admin permissions"). Only Subscription Admin or Workspace Admin can grant it. Holders can create/edit/delete objectives, key results, confidence scores, interim target schedules, and actual key result values.[18]

N2. Workspace-level "Default project access for new users"

Each workspace carries a setting Default project access for new users with values No Access, Viewer, or Editor — default No Access. This is the only automatic-grant mechanism R054 missed: when a user is created within a workspace, they receive this default level on every project in that workspace. R054's "no automatic permission cascade" claim is still correct for parent→child propagation, but a workspace-wide default does exist as an admin-tunable seed.[17]

N3. WSAPI default-grant on user creation

The WSAPI reference page states verbatim that newly created users receive "Workspace User and Project Viewer permissions according to the subscription administrators default workspace and project." This means programmatic user creation lands the new user in the subscription admin's own default workspace and default project at Viewer level — the new user is never created in a permission vacuum.[15]

N4. "Can Create API Key" is a fifth per-user permission

Subscription Admins can flip a subscription-level toggle Limit API Key Creation to Allowed Users (under Setup → Subscription → Admin Capabilities). When enabled, only users with a per-user Can Create API Key permission may mint API keys. Notable quirk: "Subscription/Workspace Administrators … cannot self-assign this permission (no access to own Permissions tab). Workaround: Apply permission via Setup → Users list using Show Fields." This is an example of Rally splitting privilege grants out of the main role tiers into orthogonal per-user toggles.[19]

N5. Re-parenting projects requires same-workspace

An existing top-level project can be converted into a child of another project via Project picker → Setup → Details tab → Parent Team field. The hard constraint: "Both projects must be in the same workspace to make changes to the hierarchy." The Broadcom doc is silent on what happens to user permissions on re-parent (no documented permission migration, no warning) — added to Gaps below.[20]

N6. Workspace deletion is impossible — only closure

Verbatim from the Workspace Admin capability page: "You cannot delete workspaces or projects. However, closing projects or workspaces can have the same effect." Workspaces are append-only at the structural level. R054 did not call this out; it has direct implications for any Vector "archive vs delete" policy.[16]

N7. New workspace auto-creates a sample project

Verbatim: "When you create a new workspace, it automatically creates a sample project within the workspace." A workspace is therefore never empty at creation time — there is always at least one node in the project tree.[14]

N8. Multi-select on "Add Existing User" is name-restricted

The project-pivot add flow (Project picker → Setup → Users tab → Add existing User) supports adding multiple users in one modal — but only if they "share a common name element" (the modal is a search-filter pattern, not a free multi-pick). Single-project scope only; no multi-project apply.[14]

N9. First-login role chooser drives the dashboard, not access

On first login a new user is prompted to pick one of three roles — Contributors (developers, testers, designers), Organizers (project managers, product owners, Scrum Masters), Executives (program managers, directors). The pick configures the default dashboard apps (e.g. Contributors get "My Tasks", "My Test Cases", "My Defects") but does not affect project or workspace permissions. Worth noting because the term "role" overlaps awkwardly with the permission-tier vocabulary.[22]

CORRECTION — R054 claims that needed amending

C1. Bulk toolkit does NOT use WSAPI for permission writes

R054 originally said the Rally-User-Management toolkit "consumes CSV templates against the WSAPI" (Bulk operations & CSV section). The WSAPI reference page directly contradicts this for permission writes — verbatim: "UserPermission will be enhanced in the future to allow modification of user permissions", and the change-permissions workflow currently "requires the graphical applications in Rally tools". So the WSAPI exposes read on UserPermission/WorkspacePermission/ProjectPermission objects but cannot write them. The Ruby toolkit therefore must be driving the web UI (or a private/internal endpoint) for permission grants — not the public WSAPI. The original Bulk-ops sentence has been amended in place to reflect this; the user-CREATE call (POST /slm/webservice/v2.0/user/create) does work as documented.[15]

C2. Admin-tier table missing OKR Admin and "Can Create API Key"

R054's "Admin tiers — who can do what" table only lists Subscription / Workspace / Project / User tiers. The Broadcom docs make clear there are at least two additional orthogonal per-user permission flags — OKR Admin and Can Create API Key — that sit outside the four-tier hierarchy and are administered from the same Permissions tab on the User Detail Editor. R054's table is not wrong, but it is incomplete; treat the four tiers as the structural model and these per-user flags as a separate "feature-permission" axis. (See N1 and N4.)

C3. Default permission for new user creation

R054 implies the per-user permission default is "No Access" everywhere unless explicitly set (Project Permission row's "No visibility to the project (Default value for each user)"). That is true for ad-hoc users, but the WSAPI page and the Customize Workspace Details page together establish two stronger seeds: (a) WSAPI-created users get Workspace User + Project Viewer on the creator's defaults (N3), and (b) any user created inside a workspace inherits that workspace's Default project access for new users setting (N2). The "No Access default" is a per-row default once the user record exists, not a per-user-creation default.

CONFIRMATION — citations to add to claims R054 already made

R054 claimNew corroborating source
Project Admin can add existing users; can only create new users if the subscription admin has delegated that"Add Existing User(s) to a Project" page[14] — verbatim: "Project administrators can only create new users if the subscription administrator has granted this permission"
Subscription Admin must be the one to create new workspaces"Create a New Workspace" page already cited as [9]; the Administration overview[21] repeats it
Project hierarchy has no documented depth cap (R054 listed this in Gaps)Confirmed — "Understanding the Rally Project Hierarchy"[10] and "Structuring Your Project Tree"[23] both contain best-practice prose ("Start with a simple structure …") with no numerical cap
"Setup icon displays next to projects where you have permission to edit" — gates the alt-paths flowConfirmed verbatim on the re-parenting page[20]
Workspace Admins manage tags, fields, work rules and full work-item rights inside their workspaceConfirmed verbatim on Managing Your Workspace[16]: "You have full rights to all work items in the workspace"

Updates to the Gaps register

  • Re-parenting permission semantics still undocumented. The "Change an Existing Project to a Child Project" page[20] describes the mechanical UI flow but is silent on whether the project's existing user-permission rows are preserved, replaced, or merged with the new parent's. This is a significant gap for any Vector implementation that needs to replicate the operation.
  • Search endpoint is a JavaScript SPA. The Rally Help search at rally-help/search.html?q=… renders results client-side; the static HTML returned to a fetcher contains only the page chrome. All Broadcom-doc enumeration in this addendum was therefore done via site:techdocs.broadcom.com web-search queries plus direct page fetches.
  • WSAPI permission-write status is "future". Verbatim on the WSAPI reference page[15]: "UserPermission will be enhanced in the future to allow modification of user permissions" — that future has not arrived. Any integration depending on programmatic permission grants must drive the web UI or use the unofficial RallyTools toolkit.

Gaps & limitations

  • No screenshots in fetched docs. Broadcom’s TechDocs pages did not surface inline screenshots through the WebFetch text extractor. The visual layout in the diagram above is a reconstruction from the verbal pane descriptions ("left-hand pane lists Workspaces", "right-hand pane lists Projects"), not from a captured image.
  • Project hierarchy depth not officially capped. The "Understanding the Rally Project Hierarchy" page recommends starting simple but does not state an explicit maximum depth. The WSAPI does not document a depth limit either.
  • Visual tree shape in the right pane is inferred. The docs say the right pane lists projects within the workspace; they do NOT explicitly state whether projects are rendered with tree-indentation or as a flat list. The reconstruction assumes indented tree rendering because the project model itself is hierarchical and that is the most usable shape — but this should be confirmed against a live screenshot.
  • Subscription Admin promotion screen. The Set User Access Permissions page does not detail where the Subscription Admin flag is toggled (Profile tab? Separate section?). A subscription-admin-only screen exists but its location was not in the fetched pages.
  • Move-user-between-workspaces semantics. Adding/removing workspace permissions is documented; what happens to a user’s in-flight project assignments when their workspace permission is downgraded to No Access (silent revoke vs explicit confirmation) is not explicitly documented in the fetched pages.
  • knowledge.broadcom.com gating. Several knowledge-base articles return content but require login for full detail; some KB content was paraphrased rather than quoted.
  • Doc currency. Broadcom TechDocs pages for Rally are largely undated; findings are considered current as of May 2026 based on the canonical valueops/rally/rally-help URL structure (the modern path — the older agile-development-and-management/rally-platform-ca-agile-central path still serves duplicate content).
